annotations.label: Limit inferior and limit superior of a sequence

anchor.snippet: The limit inferior of a sequence

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `Filter.limsup` and `Filter.liminf` for any function on a conditionally complete lattice; the sequence case is the specialization to the filter `atTop` on ℕ.

annotations.label: Subsequential limit

anchor.snippet: is a subsequential limit of

annotations.note: Mathlib has `MapClusterPt` for cluster points along a filter and `Filter.exists_seq_tendsto` extracting subsequences, but no direct standalone `IsSubsequentialLimit` predicate.

annotations.label: Existence in extended reals

anchor.snippet: the limit superior and limit inferior always exist

annotations.note: Existence is by construction in any conditionally complete lattice (and trivially in `EReal`), but there is no separate theorem statement asserting existence.

annotations.label: Agreement with ordinary limit

anchor.snippet: Whenever the ordinary limit exists, the limit inferior and limit superior are both equal to it

annotations.note: `Filter.Tendsto.limsup_eq` and `Filter.Tendsto.liminf_eq` give `limsup u f = a` and `liminf u f = a` whenever `Tendsto u f (𝓝 a)`.

annotations.label: Interpretation of limit superior

anchor.snippet: The limit superior of

annotations.note: `Filter.limsup_eq` states `limsup u f = sInf { a | ∀ᶠ n in f, u n ≤ a }`, the eventually-bounded-above characterization.

annotations.label: Interpretation of limit inferior

anchor.snippet: The limit inferior of

annotations.note: `Filter.liminf_eq` gives `liminf u f = sSup { a | ∀ᶠ n in f, a ≤ u n }`, the dual characterization.

annotations.label: Relationship between liminf and limsup

anchor.snippet: The relationship of limit inferior and limit superior for sequences of real numbers is as follows

annotations.note: `Filter.liminf_le_limsup` directly states `liminf u f ≤ limsup u f` for any non-trivial filter.

anchors.snippet: The relationship of limit inferior and limit superior for sequences of real numbers is as follows

annotations.label: Convergence iff liminf equals limsup

anchor.snippet: converges if and only if

annotations.note: Combined with `Filter.Tendsto.liminf_eq`/`.limsup_eq`, `tendsto_of_liminf_eq_limsup` provides the if-and-only-if characterization of convergence.

annotations.label: Smallest closed interval property

anchor.snippet: the interval

annotations.note: The component frequently/eventually inequalities (e.g. `frequently_lt_of_lt_limsup`, `eventually_lt_of_limsup_lt`) exist, but no single statement packages the smallest closed-interval property.

annotations.label: Finite vs infinite count above/below

anchor.snippet: If

annotations.note: The frequently/eventually characterization is captured by `frequently_lt_of_lt_limsup` and `eventually_lt_of_limsup_lt` (with `atTop`, frequently = infinitely many, eventually = cofinitely many).

annotations.label: Converse counting characterization

anchor.snippet: Conversely, it can also be shown that

annotations.note: The converse is implicit in the `sInf`/`sSup` characterization (`limsup_eq`), but no explicit standalone lemma packages this counting converse.

annotations.label: Liminf and limsup as extremal cluster points

anchor.snippet: The liminf and limsup of a sequence are respectively the smallest and greatest cluster points

annotations.note: `isGreatest_mapClusterPt_limsup` and `isLeast_mapClusterPt_liminf` state that limsup/liminf are the greatest/least map-cluster-points of the sequence along the filter.

anchors.snippet: The liminf and limsup of a sequence are respectively the smallest and greatest cluster points

annotations.label: Subadditivity of limit superior

anchor.snippet: the limit superior satisfies subadditivity

annotations.note: `Filter.limsup_add_le` proves `limsup (u + v) ≤ limsup u + limsup v`.

annotations.label: Superadditivity of limit inferior

anchor.snippet: the limit inferior satisfies superadditivity

annotations.note: `Filter.le_liminf_add` proves `liminf u + liminf v ≤ liminf (u + v)`.

anchor.snippet: For any two sequences of non-negative real numbers

annotations.note: `Filter.limsup_mul_le`, `Filter.le_liminf_mul`, and `Filter.liminf_mul_le` provide the multiplicative analogues for non-negative functions.

annotations.label: Oscillation of a function at a point

anchor.snippet: it is called the oscillation of f at 0

annotations.note: `oscillation` and `oscillationWithin` are defined in `Mathlib/Analysis/Oscillation.lean` as the EMetric diameter limit.

annotations.label: Limit superior and limit inferior of a real-valued function

anchor.snippet: The limit superior of a real-valued function defined on an interval containing a point

annotations.note: `Filter.limsup f (𝓝[≠] x)` (or `𝓝 x`) realises the limsup of a function at a point as a special case.

annotations.label: Limsup and liminf on metric spaces

anchor.snippet: There is a notion of limsup and liminf for functions defined on a metric space

annotations.note: Captured by `Filter.limsup f (𝓝 x)` on metric (in fact topological) spaces.

annotations.label: Monotonicity in epsilon

anchor.snippet: as ε shrinks, the supremum of the function over the ball is non-increasing

annotations.note: Monotonicity in ε is used inside the `iInf` defining `oscillation`, but no standalone monotonicity lemma is exposed.

annotations.label: Reformulation via sequences

anchor.snippet: The definition of limsup and liminf in a metric spaces can be equivalently reformulated as follows

annotations.note: `exists_seq_tendsto_limsup`/`liminf` give sequence-based realisations, but a full bidirectional reformulation theorem is not isolated.

annotations.label: Limsup and liminf on topological spaces

anchor.snippet: we replace metric balls with neighborhoods

annotations.note: Mathlib's filter-based `Filter.limsup`/`Filter.liminf` directly subsume the neighbourhood-filter formulation.

annotations.label: Outer limit of a sequence of sets

anchor.snippet: which is also called the outer limit

annotations.note: For `s : ℕ → Set α`, `Filter.limsup s atTop` is the outer limit; `Filter.cofinite.limsup_set_eq` confirms it as `{x | {n | x ∈ s n}.Infinite}`.

annotations.label: Inner limit of a sequence of sets

anchor.snippet: which is also called the inner limit

annotations.note: `Filter.liminf s atTop` is the inner limit; `cofinite.liminf_set_eq` gives `{x | {n | x ∉ s n}.Finite}`.

annotations.label: Set limit existence criterion

anchor.snippet: exists if and only if

annotations.note: The general criterion `liminf = limsup ↔ tendsto` (`tendsto_of_liminf_eq_limsup`) applies, but no specific set-convergence packaging is provided.

annotations.label: Discrete metric

anchor.snippet: the discrete metric is defined by

annotations.note: Mathlib has `DiscreteTopology` and several discrete instances, but no canonical `discreteMetric` definition (only the topology side and `hammingDist`-style examples).

annotations.label: Set-theoretic limsup and liminf

anchor.snippet: is a sequence of subsets of

annotations.note: Applied to `s : ℕ → Set α` with `atTop`, `Filter.limsup`/`liminf` give the set-theoretic limsup/liminf (`⋂ₙ ⋃_{k≥n} s_k` and dually).

annotations.label: Complementation identity

anchor.snippet: Observe that

annotations.note: `Filter.limsup_compl` (and dually `liminf_compl`) state `(limsup u f)ᶜ = liminf (compl ∘ u) f`, specializing to the set complementation identity.

annotations.label: Set limit existence (discrete case)

anchor.snippet: exists if and only if

annotations.note: Recoverable from the general `liminf = limsup ↔ tendsto`, but no dedicated discrete-case lemma.

annotations.label: Meet of nth tail

anchor.snippet: Let I n be the meet of the n th tail of the sequence

annotations.note: The expression `⨅ i ≥ n, s i` (meet of the nth tail) appears inside `liminf_eq_iSup_iInf_of_nat`, but is not given a named definition `I_n`.

annotations.label: Join of nth tail

anchor.snippet: let J n be the join of the n th tail of the sequence

annotations.note: Similarly `⨆ i ≥ n, s i` shows up inside `limsup_eq_iInf_iSup_of_nat` without a standalone `J_n` definition.

annotations.label: Omega limit set of a dynamic system

anchor.snippet: The Ω limit (i.e., limit set ) of a solution to a dynamic system is the outer limit of solution trajectories of the system

annotations.note: `omegaLimit f ϕ s` is defined in `Mathlib/Dynamics/OmegaLimit.lean` as the outer limit of trajectories along a filter.

annotations.label: Limit inferior of a set

anchor.snippet: The limit inferior of a set X ⊆ Y is the infimum of all of the limit points of the set

annotations.note: No grep hit for an inf-of-limit-points definition associated to a single set in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Limit superior of a set

anchor.snippet: the limit superior of X is the supremum of all of the limit points of the set

annotations.note: Likewise, no sup-of-limit-points definition for a single set is present.

annotations.label: Cluster points of a filter base

anchor.snippet: The set of all cluster points for that filter base is given by

annotations.note: Mathlib uses `ClusterPt` for cluster points of a filter; a `HasBasis`-driven set-of-cluster-points definition exists implicitly but not as a single named decl.

annotations.label: Limit superior of a filter base

anchor.snippet: The limit superior of the filter base B is defined as

annotations.note: `Filter.limsSup f = sInf { a | ∀ᶠ n in f, n ≤ a }` is exactly the filter-base limsup.

annotations.label: Limit inferior of a filter base

anchor.snippet: the limit inferior of the filter base B is defined as

annotations.note: `Filter.limsInf` dually realises the filter-base liminf.

annotations.label: Filter base limit existence

anchor.snippet: If the limit inferior and limit superior agree, then there must be exactly one cluster point

annotations.note: `le_nhds_of_limsSup_eq_limsInf` shows equality of `limsSup` and `limsInf` implies convergence; the unique-cluster-point reformulation isn't a single packaged theorem.

annotations.label: Filter base of tails of a net

anchor.snippet: The filter base ("of tails") generated by this net is

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Filter.atTop` on a preorder captures the tails filter (for nets indexed by a directed set), though Mathlib formalises convergence of nets via filters rather than naming `tails`.

annotations.label: Filter base of tails of a sequence

anchor.snippet: The filter base ("of tails") generated by this sequence is

annotations.note: `Filter.atTop` on ℕ is exactly the filter generated by the tails `{k | k ≥ n}` of a sequence.
